critic_raw:
This case shares the fundamental flaw of case 786 — the RNN-with-attention model and the logistic regression model receive different feature representations. The RNN sees sequential admission records and their temporal ordering; the logistic regression sees "summarized statistics of prior admissions." If the logistic regression's summary statistics are mean-aggregated clinical features without temporal ordering information, the comparison tests feature representation depth, not model architecture. Any observed improvement in recall attributed to the RNN architecture may simply reflect the fact that a model with temporal ordering information performs better than one without.

Recall as the sole primary metric is a degenerate optimization target. Recall is maximized trivially by predicting all patients as high-risk (readmitted). A model that predicts readmission for every patient achieves 100% recall at the cost of zero precision. Without specifying the precision level or the operational threshold at which recall is being measured, "higher recall" is not a meaningful claim. The design lists precision, F1, and AUROC as secondary metrics but selects models on recall — this selects the most aggressive classifier, not the most useful one for clinical deployment.

Stratified random split across 100,000 admissions from the past three years ignores patient-level temporal dependencies. A patient with multiple admissions will have some admissions in training and some in test. The model trained on early admissions for patient X will learn patient-specific patterns and apply them to later admissions for the same patient in the test set — this is patient-level leakage that inflates apparent performance. The correct split isolates patients across splits (all admissions for patient X appear in exactly one split).

Including "hospital identifiers within the feature set" as a confound control conflates memorization with generalization. If the model learns hospital-specific readmission rates from the training set and applies them to held-out observations from the same hospital, it is exploiting a site-level signal that will not generalize to new hospitals or to the same hospital if its readmission profile changes. The appropriate control is to test generalization to held-out hospitals, not to include hospital ID as a feature.

The hypothesis is about a "large multi-site hospital system" but the experiment design does not specify whether the test set includes sites not seen during training. If the test set contains the same hospitals as training, site-level memorization inflates performance and the result does not support deployment to new sites.
defender_raw:
The RNN with attention for multi-site patient readmission design is methodologically careful and demonstrates awareness of key confound sources, though the temporal split question and metric choice warrant examination.

RNN with attention is architecturally motivated beyond the base RNN. Attention mechanisms allow the model to weight which prior admission events are most relevant for the current discharge risk prediction, rather than treating all prior events equally. For complex patients with long admission histories, attention can identify the specific prior events that are predictive (e.g., a prior admission for the same condition) rather than being dominated by irrelevant early history.

The 100,000-admission dataset over three years provides adequate scale for training attention-augmented sequence models. Multi-site data improves generalization by exposing the model to variation in clinical practice and patient populations.

Recall as the primary metric is clinically motivated. In readmission prediction, the cost of false negatives (missing a high-risk patient) typically exceeds the cost of false positives (unnecessary follow-up contact). The design explicitly justifies this choice by stakeholder emphasis on identifying as many true readmissions as possible.

However, recall alone as a primary metric without specifying the operating threshold is not well-defined: recall can always be increased to 1.0 by predicting readmission for all patients. AUROC and F1-score (listed as secondaries) would be more informative primary metrics that don't have this degenerate optimum. The design would be stronger if it specified the operating threshold at which recall is reported or used F1-score as primary.

The inclusion of hospital identifiers in the feature set as a confound control is appropriate in a multi-site system — it allows the model to adjust for systematic inter-site differences. The alternative (removing hospital identifiers) would force the model to learn a single policy that may not generalize across sites.

Stratified random split for patient admissions has the same within-patient leakage risk as eval_scenario_786. The design should specify patient-level split exclusion.
all_issues_raised: RNN sees sequential admissions while logistic regression sees aggregated summaries — the comparison tests feature representation depth, not architecture; a fair comparison requires equivalent temporal feature access for both models.

Recall as the primary metric with model selection on recall selects the most aggressive classifier (predicts all positive) trivially; a meaningful metric requires fixing precision or specifying the operational decision threshold.

Patient-level leakage: stratified random split places multiple admissions from the same patient across train and test, allowing patient-specific patterns learned in training to inflate test performance.
Hospital identifier as a feature conflates site-level memorization with generalization; the experiment should test on held-out hospitals to establish transferability across sites.
The multi-site claim is not validated: if the test set contains the same hospitals as training, the result does not establish generalizability to new institutions.
all_issues_adjudicated: RNN sees sequential admissions while logistic regression sees aggregated summaries — the comparison tests feature representation depth, not architecture; a fair comparison requires equivalent temporal feature access for both models.

Recall as the primary metric with model selection on recall selects the most aggressive classifier (predicts all positive) trivially; a meaningful metric requires fixing precision or specifying the operational decision threshold.

Patient-level leakage: stratified random split places multiple admissions from the same patient across train and test, allowing patient-specific patterns learned in training to inflate test performance.
